name: Fountain Green
short_description: BLM public land near Fountain Green offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and quartz specimens. No permit required - perfect for Utah collectors seeking colorful chalcedony deposits.
description:
Fountain Green sits on productive BLM land in Sanpete County, Utah, where recreational rockhounds can collect agate, jasper, and quartz without permits or fees. The area is part of the Great Basin province, where ancient volcanic and sedimentary activity created extensive silica-rich formations. Collectors often find banded agates, colorful jasper pieces, and clear quartz crystals weathering out of hillsides and along washes. The terrain is relatively gentle for a Utah rockhounding site, making it accessible to families and beginners while still offering quality specimens for experienced collectors.

The geology here reflects Utah's complex mineral heritage. Miocene-era volcanic activity and subsequent weathering exposed chalcedony-bearing deposits that continue to yield excellent finds. Many specimens show beautiful banding and color variation typical of Utah's agate and jasper. The BLM designation means you have legal access to search publicly managed land under standard recreational collecting rules.

Visit during fall or spring for the most comfortable collecting conditions. Summer heat can be intense, and winter weather occasionally makes roads less accessible. The site works well for day trips from nearby towns - bring plenty of water, wear sturdy boots for rocky terrain, and plan to spend 2-4 hours exploring different hillside areas and wash deposits.
history: This region of Sanpete County lies within the Basin and Range geologic province, where Miocene volcanic activity (20-25 million years ago) deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into agate, chalcedony, and jasper nodules. Subsequent uplift, erosion, and weathering have exposed these deposits on hillsides and in washes, making them accessible to modern rockhounders.
tips: Search along dry washes and hillside erosion areas where water has naturally exposed specimens; look for the characteristic banded patterns and translucent edges of agate. Bring a 10x hand lens to examine chalcedony color layering and identify higher-quality pieces before packing them. Focus on south and southwest-facing slopes where weathering exposes fresh material - avoid collecting loose rock that may contain loose material overhead. Bring at least 3 liters of water, sunscreen, and a digging bar or small pick to extract embedded specimens from softer host rock.
terrain_notes: Rolling high desert terrain at approximately 5,300-5,600 feet elevation with scattered sagebrush, juniper, and pinyon vegetation. Terrain consists of gentle to moderate slopes with exposed rocky hillsides and dry washes. Walking distance from parking areas is typically 0.5-2 miles depending on which collecting zone you access. Footing is rocky and uneven in places; good hiking boots are essential. Some areas have loose surface rock that requires careful stepping.
best_season: April - May and September - October. Spring brings moderate temperatures ideal for hiking and collecting, while fall offers cooler conditions and clear skies. Summer (June-August) brings intense heat that can exceed 85-90 degrees F, making midday collecting uncomfortable. Winter (November-March) occasionally brings snow or icy conditions that can affect road access.
written_directions: From Fountain Green town center, head north on State Route 25 approximately 4 miles. Look for BLM access points marked by standard brown public land signs on the east side of the highway. Turn onto rough dirt roads leading into the sagebrush foothills - high-clearance vehicles recommended but passenger cars can access some areas during dry seasons. Park at informal pullouts along the dirt roads and hike into designated collecting areas. GPS coordinates (39.625905, -111.573466) mark a primary collecting zone accessible via these secondary roads.
road_conditions: Rough dirt and gravel roads with washboard texture in places. Passenger vehicles can access during dry conditions, but high-clearance or AWD recommended after rain. Some sections may have ruts or soft spots.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along dirt roads at trailheads. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles per location. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. BLM recreational collection permitted without advance authorization.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lbs maximum per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation equipment
No explosives or heavy machinery
Leave no trash
Respect private property boundaries
nearest_services: Fountain Green has limited services; Ephraim, Utah (8 miles south) offers gas, food, and supplies. Manti (12 miles south) has a larger selection of stores and restaurants.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle terrain with moderate slopes accessible to most hikers. Uneven rocky footing requires sturdy boots and caution. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Informal parking pullouts are ground-level. Collectors with mobility limitations can focus on lower-elevation wash areas near parking.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Fountain Green, Utah?
faq.answer: Fountain Green BLM land produces high-quality agate, jasper, quartz, and chalcedony specimens. Agates often display beautiful banding in reds, browns, and whites, while jasper pieces range from solid colors to patterned varieties. Clear quartz crystals are common, making this site excellent for collectors of all levels.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Fountain Green?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Fountain Green sits on BLM public land where recreational collecting is allowed under standard FLPMA personal-use rules. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day at no cost, making it an affordable option compared to fee-dig sites.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Fountain Green?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are ideal. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures (60-75 degrees F) for hiking and collecting. Avoid summer heat (85-90+ degrees F) and winter snow that can affect road access. Early morning starts are recommended year-round.

faq.question: How far is Fountain Green rockhounding from nearby towns and services?
faq.answer: Fountain Green is 8 miles north of Ephraim and 12 miles from Manti, both offering gas, food, and supplies. The collecting areas themselves are 4-6 miles north of town via State Route 25, making it a quick day trip from central Utah.

faq.question: Is Fountain Green BLM rockhounding site good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is beginner and family-friendly with easy-to-moderate terrain, free access, and good specimen quality. Kids age 6+ can participate in collecting. The gentle slopes and accessible wash areas make it less physically demanding than many Utah rockhounding sites.

faq.question: What tools and gear do you need for rockhounding at Fountain Green?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, at least 3 liters of water, sunscreen, and a 10x hand lens for inspecting specimens. A digging bar, small pick, or chisel helps extract agate and jasper from host rock. A backpack for carrying 25 lbs of specimens and a GPS unit are helpful. Work gloves protect hands from sharp material.

meta_title: Fountain Green Rockhounding - Free Utah Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Hunt for agate, jasper, and quartz at Fountain Green BLM land near Ephraim, Utah. Free public rockhounding, no permit required. Easy terrain perfect for beginners.
